speaker: interviewer
text: We're doing an interview with Gershon Wilkenfeld, and today is the 29th of March, 1995. Would you like to continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So we're in Hamburg, in this environment-- and, of course, you know, I've got photos I'm showing you-- we had a variety, a mix of children. They are coming from all sorts of background, and so the religious part didn't really play a-- how will I say-- a big part in it. However--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that bother you at all? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I felt it in me, but on the other hand, now, it was a question of survival more than which was more important. Because, you see, at this point now, it started to come out that we were not allowed to enter into shops. We were being discriminated against. You know, when there was food on the table, we ate what there was. We didn't ask. You know, I must say this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the situation--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what you weren't allowed to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the situation comes to that, and you're-- you're going to say, well, I'm going to be fussy. I'm going to-- I wasn't the type to say, I'm going to rather starve to death than eat non-kosher food or anything like that. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, I guess it was a natural thing that, once you get away from your parental environment, the normal environment, that you tend to go with the-- with the tide. You know? It's difficult to go out of your way, under those circumstances, and go and try and find, in-- in 1939, in 1940 now, in Hamburg, kosher food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it's just impossible, particularly under the circumstances when, like I said, you're discriminated against. But I want to come back to this. Let me just come back to what happened, and maybe this will come out anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were sent to a Jewish school, like I mentioned before, in [INAUDIBLE]. And now the time had come-- time had marched on, and I think we're now in 1940. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The war had progressed, the Germans had marched into Poland in the meantime, and there was the bombing going on of England with the rockets and all this. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on this one night, we heard the sirens going, and, of course, there had been drilling-- drilling in the sense of fire drills going on-- and in our building, every building had to have wardens that would look after security with-- they had to be-- you know, all the things that go with war time drill and all that sort of thing. And we partook in all that. We were part of it. Anyway, on this day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the sirens started to wail. We went into the cellars. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the building where you lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the building--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were there, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: 50? Mainly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Children.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Half a dozen girls, which were about 16, 17, about two or three years older than us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in charge, or they were just a part of the group?

words.text: or they were just a part

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were just part of the group, but in sort of-- obviously in separate room. I don't know what the reason was for those girls to be there. I mean, if there was any other reason, I can't tell you that. But all the others, I was amongst the oldest of the children there, and I was in my 13th year. And I'm repeating that because I'll tell you--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's important, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that's important for a reason which you'll-- you'll see later. Anyway, the bombing raid took place and, of course, next day when we went to go to school, what happened was the first bombs that fell on Hamburg destroyed our school. So it was a bit-- we thought it peculiar to see that the English go to destroy German whatever, and the first thing they should hit is a Jewish school. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- you arrived at school the next day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was no school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] school and there was no school there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the end of our going to school. What was arranged afterwards that we had private teachers come in and we used to go-- and then, of course, the air raids became more frequent and more frequent, and the bombs came closer and closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I mean, I distinctly remember-- and this is some of the stupid things children or young people do, or people in danger do sometimes, and I think I mentioned to you before that in a couple of cases, but this was one of the things that-- I was in charge of-- one of the wardens that would go out the building to see if any incendiary bombs might have fallen on the building. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would stand up on the roof and we would watch these bombs falling on St. Pauli, which is a harbor area, which wasn't far away, and destroy-- start destroying these buildings and all that, not even giving a thought of it that, hey, the next one might fall on our building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were happy this was happening. You know what I'm saying?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't make sense when you think about it logically, but that was the way we were feeling, that it was good that this was taking place and--

speaker: interviewer
text: That Germany was being bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right, that, you know, maybe this--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew that your school was bombed by accident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was bombed by accident, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was bombed because the area.

speaker: interviewee
text: --But the thing is that this was only one, but, I mean, we lived with that now for months after that. And during that time, St. Pauli was completely destroyed, and Hamburg really took a hammering. And we moved around Hamburg. We were allowed only certain-- to go into certain areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only because--

speaker: interviewer
text: --identification at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not quite sure. This is a moot point. I can't remember. I really can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I seem to remember-- not a star, but a-- I seem to remember something and then I don't. You know? It's sort of gone out of my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just like-- the mind is a funny thing. After I left this place, I could not remember the address even. I just wiped it out of my mind and I don't know whether I should go into that now, but maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's up to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'll just talk about it because I might forget, and I thought it was very interesting. After the war-- no, no I won't go into it. I'll come back--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK we'll come back into it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remind me, I'll come back after.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll remind you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened here was, we kept ourselves busy. We learned Hebrew. We had all sorts of other lectures. And then some of those girls, the seven girls that I was talking about, were taken away. But one of them, who was a particularly close friend of mine, told me that they're going to try and escape to-- to Romania-- via Romania to try and go on a boat to Israel. Now these girls then left, and that thing sort of stuck in my mind, that somebody is trying to get out via other countries to get to Palestine. And while I think of it--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your first recollection of people saying that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That there were some people wanting to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: And while I'm thinking of it, whilst looking through some of my papers, I found a letter that the girls wrote to me from [INAUDIBLE] in 1941, after they heard that we had arrived, and telling us some of their experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: The girls you'd been together with in  Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had finally arrived in Palestine, and they came there on a boat which was in-- blown up in Haifa Harbor. And unfortunately, one of the girls got drowned in that incident. So she went a long way for nothing. You know, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the other ones survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Anyway, to come back to-- this was the [? patria? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, to come back to the story, what happened is, when you live in a group of young children, confined-- going on hikes every now and then, but still very confined-- you get to know people, and you get to know some of their worries and some of their things. And we were talking about families, what happened to this, what happened to that. And one thing we noticed, and one thing was very important, that in those days, still, birthdays were a-- at that age-- were an important part of your life. We started to notice a pattern, that whenever somebody's 14th birthday came near, they had to go. They were going home. They were going here. They were going there. They were going to visit an uncle. And after a while, I became suspicious, and I talked to this good friend of mine about it, and we were keeping an eye on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were almost 14 then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was coming up to 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your friend was also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- so one night, we broke into the office, and we saw-- we found, kept with meticulous--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose office was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the office of the person-- our leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was this leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in his 40's.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there just one leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just one, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he didn't live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, he was acting under orders from I don't know who.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't live there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: He just came in and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, did he or didn't he? Actually, I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he organize your food and your activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was all organized. You know, and I really know very little about it. I know some of the things we did, but sort of never--

speaker: interviewer
text: No one else?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Concerned us or--

speaker: interviewer
text: No one else around that age, or in their 20s, that helping?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just that one person. Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Hardy Plauter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hardy Plauter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hardy Plauter, P-L-A-U-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a Jewish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of about 40?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got his photo here, too. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, to-- we found out that these children were taken away on the orders of the Gestapo and sent to Sachsenhausen, which we knew to be a concentration camp, to be sent from there to other places and wherever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Sachsenhausen from Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: The point was, what we learned from that was that, hey, they're not going home. They're not going to visit their uncles and all that sort of thing, that there is a scheme in this whole situation. So we faced him, and he finally [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] owned up. Yes, there is, but please don't talk about it, because the fact is, as upset he is about it, there's nothing he can do about it. That was his point of view.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your friend just confronted him about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we talked about it and we said, OK, if we're going to sit here and, you know, we're getting close to 14th-- and it didn't take place exactly on the 14th. You know, it was around about that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 14th birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, it could be two months later, it could be at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we said, OK. We decided we're going to run away. You know, it happens even today that kids run away, so there were-- under those circumstances. The fact was, I had been through. I knew what happened to my father, that he had to disappear, and I said, OK. My time's come. I've got to make an effort.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was either-- what can happen? If I get caught, I get sent to camp with its consequences of possible-- we didn't know at that time. We knew there were camps. We knew that it wasn't a [NON-ENGLISH]. It wasn't a-- a place where you go for [INAUDIBLE]. Holiday camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Holiday camp. But we didn't know the seriousness of the situation, of the eventual--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what you thought of it at all? When you found out that the boys were going to Sachsenhausen, what did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we thought that they-- we didn't know what they're going to-- what's going to happen, but we knew it wasn't a good place to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: To be confined and all this whole thing. So we decided to run away, and we told him so. And he said, look, I can't do that because I have certain responsibilities. But if your mother will give you the OK, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then there's nothing I can do about it. So we made a call to my mother, and-- it was a difficult time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you tell me that, did you, in that whole time in Hamburg, have contact with your mother? Regular contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, regular is one-- you know, you can say regular. I used to write cards to her and I used to get a few cards back. Telephones in those days were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rare things. It was expensive, we had no money, but there was contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I called my mother-- I was lucky to get her-- and I explained the situation to her. And I said, look-- I knew I had to be careful of what I said. And I said to her, I wasn't happy here. I didn't like-- I didn't want to stay and gave all sorts of excuses why, you know, I didn't like it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, well, what do you want to do? She didn't really want me to come back home, because she wasn't allowed to leave the place. And what are you going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, I want to visit Uncle Chaim. Uncle Chaim was her brother. And there was a silence at the other side for a while, and then she said, OK. If that's what you want to do, that's fine. And of course, I must explain here that her brother, Chaim, was living in Tel Aviv. And she got the message of what I was trying to do. I mean, she knew that I'm going to give it a go and try and get out of Germany with the eventual--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a code you were trying to give her--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a code. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were going to Israel without saying you were going to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're speaking through the [INAUDIBLE]. You know, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew the phone call could be overheard.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a type of language that was used not to-- not to spell it out, because, I mean, I was-- I would have given myself away if I would have said I want to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she agreed. He listened in, and he said, OK, if she says that's fine. So we then started making a few inquiries, and I must say he was helpful with that, and put us onto an address in Berlin that when we were going to go. And we just packed a little parcel for ourselves. The date had come, I advised my mother, and by pure coincidence, my late mother had asked for permission to leave Aken to visit Berlin, to fix up something, and it coincided on the day that-- I knew when she was going to be there and I left Hamburg with this friend of mine, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vitus.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-I-T-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mandelbaum. And we arrived in Berlin, and I met my mother at the station there. No, sorry. I met her at my aunt's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin. I had an aunt living there. My mother took me to the station when we were leaving. And that's the last time I saw them, that was-- together with my brother and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had your brother and sister with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and she went back to Aken and then, we went on from there. But I must say here, we met-- we got in touch with an organization in Berlin that was taking care of homeless people-- Jewish people, or people in need-- and I can't remember the title of it. But we were put in touch with an organization that was attempting and giving guidance to young children to escape, and, of course, helping them to do so. An organization which was led by a lady [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the name of Recha Freier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-E-C-H-A, Recha. Freier. I think it was F-R-E-I-E-R. She was the wife of a Berlin rabbi. I believe they were separated. But she had taken it up on herself--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: She would have been in her 40s. To me, at the time, she looked like an old lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, it's also the way she dressed. She always had long dresses on and all this sort of thing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must add here, there was another aspect which was rather important. Recha had taken it upon herself to, particularly, take children who were of originally Polish background. Because for some peculiar reasons, whenever there were places-- like, for instance, there were places given to young children transports to go to Switzerland or to Sweden-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the preference was always given to young German Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there was a distinction-- not very nice to talk about it, but it was there, nevertheless, amongst Jewish people-- between Ost Juden and their offspring, and the German Jews. You know what I mean? Recha recognized that. And she took-- Ost Juden meaning--

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning Jews coming from the east.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. From any eastern countries like Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were, [INAUDIBLE] at the time. They were looked down, not unlike what happened when the [INAUDIBLE] came to this country by the establishment Jews in Australia. You know the history a little bit about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's an unfortunate situation, but it's true, nevertheless. So she had taken it upon herself to particularly look after, or give preference, to those children. Not exclusively, but anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were put in touch with 10 other young children, the youngest of whom was six years old. His parents had been taken to a concentration camp. The only person he had left was an uncle in Tel Aviv, and the kid was by himself. So he was given-- we were taking charge of him. Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- he was German, but I don't remember where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a German kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he knew that his parents had been taken away? You were aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he told us. We were told by these people that he had told them and--

speaker: interviewer
text: The six-year-old?

speaker: interviewee
text: The aim was to-- yeah. The aim was to get him to-- to Palestine. So here we are in Berlin. We're given a time. We're told, now, this and this is what you do, and what you do is you try and make your way to Vienna. In Vienna, you will be met by somebody who will guide you further. And then that's what took place. So we left Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Berlin at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, only for maybe a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living at your aunties place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't even a week. It was about four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- my mother was at the station. We said goodbye, and we left there together with an adult lady who accompanied us to Vienna with her daughter. And I'm not quite sure. She was sort of a chaperone, if you like. But we were really told beforehand, yes, she's your chaperone. Listen to her, you know, if you can. On the other hand, remember you're on your own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was given to all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: To all of you [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, when the time comes-- you know, you're going as a group, but each one for himself, type of thing. You know, try and help each other, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had meetings to that effect in those few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, we had-- we had briefings--

speaker: interviewer
text: Briefings?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Beforehand of this is what we were going to do. And this was not-- we were not the only group. I mean, there were-- you know, there were people-- this was an organization that was going on all the time. Of course, it was very much improved on later on-- in later war years where people, like Hannah Szenes and other people, you know, parachuted in behind the lines and all that to get people out. But this was the beginning of it. And so we proceeded to Vienna, we got there--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did take with you from Berlin at that point of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you? You and your friend? Did you take a little bag?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a little-- just a little satchel. You know, a change of clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for an idea of what was in it, just one change of clothes? Some underwear?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of shirts and a bit of underwear and, you know, a spare pair of shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were some personal things. I mean, I had my-- my prayer shawl. I'm saying it in English-- a Tallit. And tefillin and, you know, some photographs. And-- and my mother, I remember, gave me some gold coinage that she had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not actual money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was some gold coins, which was money, if you like. But it was just to have something of value in case I might need it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what the other kids had in their thing, you know, but it was-- wasn't a big deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got to Vienna. We were met by the person. We then went from Vienna by bus to Graz, which is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's Graz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Graz. [SIGHS] I'm not quite sure now. Maybe I should have looked it up. It's about 40 kilometers out towards the-- towards the Alps, but towards the Yugoslav border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how it's spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. G-R-A-Z. If you like, you can look it up after. So there, again, we were met by-- an organized-- like, and organized into a group, which we had already-- but by a guide. And this guide, his job was to try and get us across the border-- that is, the Austrian border-- into Yugoslavia. Now this is December, 1940. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's winter time. It's cold, and something happened there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you taken a warm coat with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. We had a leather jacket. We-- something happened. There were two guides, and I think one of them disappeared. But anyway, that doesn't matter. We went with our guide and we started--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were in the group at that--

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 of you at that point of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we started walking, and we walked for not only days but weeks, you know, in the Alps. And this guy used to take us-- and he had connections. He would find-- talk to farms that used to be up and he used to know huts where we could stay overnight. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just walked and slept wherever you could?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was a question of just getting across the Alps. We finally got to the German border, and then he had to work out a time, because the border guards were changing at certain times and the thing was to get across the border when the change of guard was taking place. And then, from the border, you would get in-- we were told and it was so-- we would get into an area which was called a no man's land. I don't know exactly how deep it was. And then we had-- the second thing is to get into the Yugoslav, or Croatian, side border. And they were, of course, on the lookout for people as well, because they didn't want to have these people in their country. If you were caught, you were just sent back and then you either went to camp, or you-- or you got away and you tried again. Anyway, we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived under very hard circumstances in the woods. You know, sometimes with potato peels, whatever the farmers used to throw out, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: As your food and provisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And other times, tree barks and-- whatever it was that we could find.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think it was for several weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, because this was December and we didn't arrive till late January in-- on-- to the other side, which was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned-- was the youngest still with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was with us. Anyway, as we were crossing the border, the time came to-- to go, because it's impossible to just sit there and wait. We managed to get across the German border and the German guards woke up that something was moving and suddenly, they started shooting. Now, this was the middle of the night and-- I can only describe it this way. We were on a narrow icy path with a sheer cliff wall on the left side, narrow path, and an abyss on the other side. And we were walking in one line. And they started shooting. We started not running but going very fast. And the narrow path was very covered in ice and that, and a fellow, not immediately in front of me, but two in front of me, slipped. Well, at least I think he slipped. It was either slipped or he was shot. I don't know, but he fell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was shooting? Was it the Croatians or the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: He fell. The fellow behind him tried to get hold of him and got caught up with him, and the two of them just disappeared in front of me. And I don't know why but we-- I just kept walking. You know, it was pitch dark. And so we lost two of the boys, never heard of them again, never knew what actually happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were those boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: 14 As well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we got across, away from the German border. Then there was some more shooting, and, you know, anyway, we managed to get across that border. We managed to get across to the other side, and we were met there by a different guide who then, over a period of days, took us to a place called Maribor. I mean, I don't want to stay on this whole escapade day by day because--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's OK. Yeah. No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took us to a place called Maribor, which was the closest city to-- that we came to.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were in Croatia at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Croatia at this time. Now, at this stage, two cars were supposed to meet us, and this was part of the organization from Zagreb, which was probably another 100 kilometers away. Or maybe not even that. I can't remember exactly the distances.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. That's OK. You knew you were heading for Zagreb.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew our destination was Zagreb. This is where the Recha Freier was sitting, waiting for us. When I say sitting, she was-- that was where her organization was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her organization was waiting to take you to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so when we arrived in Maribor, at the appointed time, only one car turned up. Now, you can't put 10 children into one car. Five had to stay behind. The promise was that the car would come back the next day and pick us up, or another car. But it never came.

speaker: interviewer
text: It turned out-- and the driver told us, if you can find, there's a Jewish family, they are called Deutsch. Deitz? Deutsch? It was a timber merchant. He will hide you for a day or the night, and that's what happened. We stayed with that family for a week. The five of you? The other five?

speaker: interviewee
text: The five of us. And he fed us. He hid us in his timber yard. And when the time went on and there was no sight of anybody coming to pick us up, he said, look, I'm in danger myself. We can't go on like this. We have to do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are we going to do? He said, look, I suggest I'm going to buy train tickets for you. And so he did. He got five tickets, and-- We'll have a tape change and we'll--
